plainMeaning: With a calm, devoted heart I bow to Lord Ajita, who is free from fear, free from sin, free from passion, and free from disease.
recitation: abhayaṁ aṇahaṁ arayaṁ aruaṁ, ajiaṁ ajiaṁ payao paṇame.

wordByWord.gloss: the fearless one; free from fear
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit abhaya < Skt a- ('not') + bhaya ('fear'); accusative singular qualifying the Jina.
wordByWord.notes: Here an epithet of the Jina, not the noun abhaya glossed in the preface.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit aṇaha < Skt anagha = an- ('not') + agha ('sin, evil'); accusative singular.
Prakrit araya < Skt araja = a- ('without') + raja ('dust; passion, karmic taint'); accusative singular.
wordByWord.notes: Raja is read doubly as the literal dust and the 'dust' of passion-karma; some recensions gloss it via arata ('free from attachment').

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit arua < Skt aruja = a- ('without') + ruj/rujā ('disease, affliction'); accusative singular.
Prakrit ajia < Skt ajita = a- ('not') + jita ('conquered'); accusative singular.
wordByWord.notes: First of the doubled occurrence; read here as the descriptive epithet 'invincible'.

wordByWord.etymology: Same form ajia < ajita, here as the proper name of the Tīrthaṅkara; accusative singular, object of paṇame.
wordByWord.notes: The repetition fuses epithet and name — 'the unconquered Ajita' — the hymn's signature bivalence.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit payao < Skt prayataḥ ('self-restrained, pure, devout'); nominative singular describing the worshipper.
wordByWord.notes: Adverbial in force here — qualifying the manner of the bow.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit paṇame < Skt praṇamāmi = pra- (intensive) + √nam ('to bow'); 1st person singular present.

literalTranslation: The fearless, the sinless, the passionless, the disease-free — to the unconquered Ajita, self-restrained and devout, I bow.
idiomaticTranslation: To the Fearless, the Sinless, the Passionless, the Unailing — to invincible Ajita — with a heart purified and composed, I bow down.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: After the crowded tableau of the preceding stanza — asuras, garuḍas, kinnaras, serpent-spirits, a hundred crores of gods, and the whole monastic Saṅgha pressing round to venerate — this verse abruptly narrows the lens. The cosmic chorus falls silent and a single devotee speaks: 'payao paṇame' — 'I bow.' The grandeur of collective worship is distilled into one personal act of homage, and that shift from 'they revere' to 'I bow' is the verse's quiet hinge. The Jina is named through four a-privative epithets fired in quick succession — abhaya, aṇaha, araya, arua — each stripping away one limitation: fear, sin, passion, disease. The clipped, rhyming four-beat cola make the line itself feel like a swift, bowing rhythm. The doubled 'ajiaṁ ajiaṁ' then clinches it: the first carries the adjectival sense 'invincible,' the second lands as the proper name Ajita, so the epithet and the person become one breath. Crucially the worshipper is qualified too — 'payao,' self-restrained and inwardly pure — reminding the reciter that the bow counts only when the mind that offers it is composed. The verse thus models its own instruction: name the perfections, purify the heart, bow.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The four epithets sketch the Jina as the soul restored to its own nature. 'Araya' (free of raja, the dust of passion) and 'aṇaha' (free of agha, karmic sin) point to the destruction of the ghātī karmas and the rāga-dveṣa they breed — the inner victory that makes him 'ajia,' unconquered by the internal enemies. 'Abhaya' marks the fruit the Jina then bestows: fearlessness, the foremost gift (abhaya-dāna) a Tīrthaṅkara grants all beings. 'Arua,' disease-free, gestures beyond bodily health to the affliction-less state of the liberated self. That the devotee approaches 'payato' (self-restrained) reflects the Jain insistence that bhāva — inner purity of intention — is what gives an outward act (dravya) its worth.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Recited as part of the second Smaraṇa's protective sweep, this verse trains the reciter to do two things at once: hold the Jina's four freedoms before the mind as the goal, and check the quality of one's own bow. The cue 'payao' asks for a settled, restrained attitude before the praṇāma — making the prostration an act of inner composure rather than reflex.

crossReferences.text: Logassa, praise of the 24 Jinas as 'free from rāga and disease' (vīyarāya, jara-maraṇa-vippamukka)
Opening verse 'ajiyaṁ jia-savva-bhayaṁ, saṁtiṁ ca pasanta-savva-gaya-pāvaṁ' establishing the abhaya/fearlessness motif

notes: Verse text preserved verbatim from the book, including single-conjunct/anusvāra forms (અરુઅં, અજિઅં). 'અરયં' is read as araja ('passion/dust-free'); an alternative derivation from arata ('free from attachment') yields a near-identical sense and is noted as a variant. The doubled 'અજિઅં અજિઅં' is interpreted as epithet ('unconquered') + proper name (Ajita); some readings take both as emphatic adjective. 'પયઓ' (prayata) is grammatically nominative (describing the worshipper) but functions adverbially in translation. Full scholarly English editions of the complete hymn remain limited online; glosses are corroborated across the listed sources rather than a single critical edition.
